quotes.quote: And we started getting domestic violence calls, landlord disputes, problems with the police. And, you know, we're trying to answer these calls. And, you know, the domestic violence calls, we'll send some armed Panthers over there. And they would, you know, stop that domestic violence that's going on. It was really empowering. And for the first time, the community had somebody they could call.
quotes.context: Aaron Dixon on the early work of the Seattle chapter of the Black Panther Party.

quotes.quote: She said, your name means Amos. That's a Hebrew word. And it means the prophet who bears the burdens of the people. And I never forgot that.
quotes.context: Reverend Amos C. Brown on being told the meaning of his name at age eight by Mrs. Estell Randall, treasurer of the Jackson NAACP branch, at Farish Street Baptist Church in Jackson, Mississippi.

quotes.quote: But when I bought the ticket, I had bought me a knife to go with the ticket, because I'm going to protect myself on the bus. And Wilson told me I couldn't go because I didn't understand the concept of nonviolence. And I asked him what the nonviolence means. He said, this is when someone hit you, you can't hit them back. I said, I don't think I want to do that. So I got a refund for my ticket.
quotes.context: Annie Pearl Avery on trying to join the Freedom Rides as a teenager in Birmingham, Alabama in 1961.

quotes.entry_subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
quotes.quote: We were 77 days in the county jail. We were 52 days in isolation at the Reformatory School. We didn't have nobody but each other to talk to and we had a Bible they gave us.
quotes.context: Audrey Nell Hamilton, recalling her and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer's incarceration as teenagers after a sit-in at St. Augustine, Florida in July 1963.

quotes.quote: My mother, she told me never to hold back. If I have to sneeze, sneeze. If you have to laugh, laugh. Don't hold it back.
quotes.context: Bill Russell on where his famous laugh came from, opening his oral history with a memory of his mother Katie Russell.

quotes.quote: You cannot make a moral appeal in the midst of an amoral society.
quotes.context: Chuck McDew, second chairman of SNCC, on why he believed nonviolence as a way of life — as distinct from nonviolence as a tactic — would not work in the segregationist South.

quotes.quote: It seemed like every space that I had, if a white man wanted that space, I had to get out of that space and give it to him. I had to always take a step to the side or step to the back and never step forward.
quotes.context: Charles McLaurin on what drew him to voter-registration work in Mississippi in 1961.

quotes.quote: Anytime anybody in SNCC is ever injured or wounded or is in jail or whatever it is, the philosophy of the organization was that we sent somebody in right away.
quotes.context: Cleveland Sellers on SNCC's operational principle of mutual aid, recounting the response when John Lewis was beaten on Bloody Sunday in Selma.

quotes.quote: I would go to school, but as I was coming home, I would hide my books, because I would try to, you know, I kind of lived in both worlds, the world of going to school, and the world of being where, you know, people were.
quotes.context: Courtland Cox on attending Catholic school as the only Black student at St. Helena's in the Bronx and walking home to a different world in the projects.

quotes.quote: The radical sees beyond the acceptable barriers for action and conversation and bangs against the outer walls to make progress much farther than is institutionally conceived. Now, being a realist, even though you're doing that and you're trying to open new horizons beyond acceptable boundaries, the end result is that you're lucky if you get to those walls.
quotes.context: Judge D'Army Bailey, founder of the National Civil Rights Museum, on the difference between an activist and a radical.

quotes.quote: But a shotgun blast came straight through the window, passed over my body. Literally, if I would have had this girth then, a part of my body would have been shot off.
quotes.context: Dion Diamond on a near miss while staying at Mama Cotton's house in McComb, Mississippi in 1961, where he and Bob Moses were doing voter-registration work.

quotes.quote: And I thought, just imagine we can't use the public library and yet they, white folks, call us ignorant.
quotes.context: Dorothy Foreman Cotton on Reverend Wyatt Tee Walker's campaign to desegregate the public library in Petersburg, Virginia, where Cotton served as his NAACP secretary.

quotes.quote: I remember the war was living in our house. The war was another member of the family.
quotes.context: Dottie Zellner on growing up in a left-wing Jewish immigrant household in New York City during World War II, where her father ran in from his dentist's office between patients to hear the war news on the radio.

quotes.quote: No, he never had a chance to vote. His voter registration card came in time to be buried with him.
quotes.context: Ellie Dahmer on her husband Vernon Dahmer, the Hattiesburg NAACP leader who was killed in a 1966 Klan firebombing of their home the night after he announced he would help neighbors register to vote.

quotes.quote: Miss Hamer was in such pain. Her hands were all black and blue and swollen. And her buttocks were all swollen and hard. And she developed a fever. And the only thing I had was a washcloth. And we had a sink there which, you know, I put, kept putting cold water on it.
quotes.context: Euvester Simpson on caring for Fannie Lou Hamer in their shared jail cell in Winona, Mississippi, after Hamer was severely beaten by police on the night of June 9, 1963.

quotes.quote: And she said something that was so powerful. She said, Freeman, but he was somebody's child. All of what he did came because of how he was raised.
quotes.context: Freeman A. Hrabowski III on his mother's reaction to the death of Eugene "Bull" Connor, the Birmingham police commissioner who had jailed Hrabowski during the 1963 Children's Crusade when he was 12 years old.

quotes.quote: Well, we didn't ever think we were supposed to vote on whether we could go into a restaurant. We were born here, blacks in Cambridge, you'd been there for 300 and so many years, a mixture of slave, free, freed.
quotes.context: Gloria Richardson, leader of the Cambridge Nonviolent Action Committee, on refusing to endorse a public referendum on the Cambridge MD public-accommodations amendment in 1963.

quotes.quote: We discussed everything. You know, sometimes we like SNCC before me. We'd meet for hours coming to decisions. And that seemed right to me, you know. And so that form of leadership that's where everybody has to say, you try to come to consensus as opposed to just the vote unless you just cannot reach consensus.
quotes.context: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons on her style as a SNCC project director in Laurel, Mississippi during 1964 Freedom Summer.

quotes.quote: Mississippi had a law that you had to stop. You're driving. You had to stop at any set of railroad tracks before proceeding. And nobody from Mississippi paid any attention to that because there were railroad spurs all over the place and you just went over them. But if you were part of the movement, black and certainly black activists, SNCC or COFO or SCLC or MCHR or whatever. And you didn't stop. The cops would, if there were cops around, they would arrest you for violating that law.
quotes.context: Dr. H. Jack Geiger of the Medical Committee for Human Rights, on the COFO creep — slowing the car to almost a stop at railroad tracks so movement workers wouldn't be marked as outsiders.

quotes.quote: He said, John, he said to me, he said, that's a better question than last one. He said, but you know, I have to go back to Memphis and stand for those that won't stand for themselves and stand for those that can't stand for themselves.
quotes.context: John Carlos recalling Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s answer when Carlos asked him in early 1968 why he would go back to Memphis after receiving death threats.

quotes.quote: What my sister remembered was that when the guy from the national would come up and sit in my kitchen talking to my father. And what my sister said was that my mother would immediately close the curtains because we were ground level. We had a little teeny tiny little house right on the ground there. And she said she would immediately close the curtains so that nobody could see that my father was talking to somebody from the national. Because she was afraid of course with good reason that he might lose his job.
quotes.context: Judy Richardson, SNCC veteran and Eyes on the Prize producer, on her father William King Richardson's work organizing the UAW local at the Tarrytown NY GM plant in the early 1950s.

quotes.quote: I never really thought of the movement as dangerous. I guess if you commit to the movement, you accept that danger. That's part of the price, but the danger is just because you are challenging a tremendously powerful and unjust society.
quotes.context: Kathleen Cleaver, former SNCC and Black Panther Party Communications Secretary, reflecting on her early years in the movement.

quotes.quote: And they beat me almost to death. And they want me to sign a statement saying, I drove over there and fell out of a car and hurt myself. I said, I'm not going to sign there. I'd never driven a car in my life.
quotes.context: Lawrence Guyot, chair of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, on being beaten in the Winona, Mississippi jail on June 10, 1963 — the same night Fannie Lou Hamer was beaten in a cell down the hall.

quotes.quote: And then this older gentleman got up and he said, you don't know what it feels like to know that we're not the only ones.
quotes.context: Maria Varela on showing a filmstrip about the United Farm Workers to Black plantation workers in a Louisiana tent city — the workers, having been thrown off their plantations for trying to vote, had assumed that only they faced such violence.

quotes.quote: And I remember the helicopters overhead flying over the cornfields on either side. So to watch for somebody with a rifle.
quotes.context: Pete Seeger on marching from Selma to Montgomery in March 1965.

quotes.quote: We saw just a community living under the grind and the weight of segregation determined not to be terrorized or intimidated.
quotes.context: Ruby Sales on door-to-door voter registration work with SNCC in Lowndes County, Alabama in 1965.

quotes.quote: And we'd hang around that paper, hang around the home, the kid would come in and dance and doing what kids do, reading or refrigerator and that sort of thing.
quotes.context: Moses Newson, the Tri-State Defender reporter who covered the 1957 Little Rock school integration, on time spent at the home of NAACP leader Daisy Bates with the Little Rock Nine.

quotes.quote: We were adamant that you could not really change the South unless you changed the politics. That all that business of weight-ins and sit-ins and pray-ins were fine from a cosmetic point of view, but it didn't alter the status and the political situation of black people.
quotes.context: Timothy Jenkins on the 1961 debate inside SNCC between the direct-action and voter-registration factions.

quotes.quote: I'm not interested in anyone going to jail. I'm interested in knowing the facts what happened. I still want to know what happened.
quotes.context: Wheeler Parker Jr., Emmett Till's cousin, who was sleeping in the same house the night Till was abducted in Money, Mississippi in 1955, on what he still seeks decades later.

quotes.quote: But the children's march is what toppled the segregation system in Birmingham. When people saw the firemen with the hoses using them against children running them down the sidewalk, they said this won't do.
quotes.context: Reverend Wyatt Tee Walker, Dr. King's chief of staff at SCLC and architect of Project C, on the 1963 Birmingham campaign.
